title: It's A Bear Market Rally, The Selloff To Resume
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: A bear market rally is a temporary rise in stock prices during a downtrend, often followed by a return to bearish conditions. Analysts debate if recent gains si
excerpt: A bear market rally is a temporary rise in stock prices during a downtrend, often followed by a return to bearish conditions. Analysts debate if recent gains signal a bull market or just a bear market rally. The selloff is expected to resume as underlying issues persist.

content.introduction: The recent market surge has left investors questioning whether this marks the beginning of a new bull market or merely a temporary respite in an ongoing bear market. Analysts at Sigma Capital examine the evidence suggesting that current gains may be unsustainable, with fundamental weaknesses likely to drive markets lower in the coming months.
body:
## Understanding Bear Market Rallies

A bear market rally is defined as a temporary increase in stock prices during a broader downward trend, typically characterized by gains of 10% or more over a period of weeks or months. Historically, these rallies occur when oversold conditions trigger short-covering or when positive news temporarily outweighs negative fundamentals. The S&P 500 has experienced an average bear market rally of 19.3% since 1950, according to data from J.P. Morgan, with these rallies lasting an average of 52 days before the downtrend resumed. The current rally, which began in mid-June 2023, has seen the S&P 500 climb approximately 15% as of early August, raising questions about whether this time is different or if history is about to repeat itself.

## Market Fundamentals Remain Challenging

Despite the recent gains, underlying economic indicators continue to paint a concerning picture for investors. The Federal Reserve's ongoing battle with inflation has resulted in interest rates that now exceed 5%, levels not seen since 2001. This tightening cycle has historically preceded economic downturns, with the yield curve remaining deeply inverted since October 2022—a reliable predictor of recessions. Corporate earnings growth has also decelerated sharply, with Q2 2023 earnings showing just 3.5% year-over-year growth compared to 15% in the same period of 2022. Additionally, consumer sentiment remains fragile, with the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index hovering near a 10-year low, suggesting that discretionary spending—a key driver of corporate profits—may continue to weaken.

## Technical Signals Suggest Caution

Technical analysis provides further evidence that the current rally may be unsustainable. Market breadth has been deteriorating despite rising prices, with the NYSE Advance-Decline Line failing to confirm new highs—a classic bear market divergence. Additionally, put-call ratios have remained elevated, indicating persistent hedging activity by institutional investors who appear to be using the rally as an opportunity to reduce exposure. The Volatility Index (VIX), while down from its May peak of 18.5, remains elevated above 14, suggesting that investors continue to price in significant volatility. These technical divergences, combined with negative seasonality patterns that have historically shown September to be the weakest month for stocks, lead many analysts to believe that the recent rally may be setting the stage for a renewed selloff.

## Investor Sentiment and Market Psychology

The current market environment is characterized by extreme sentiment swings that often precede significant trend reversals. The American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) Bull/Bear ratio recently reached 1.6, indicating that approximately 60% of individual investors are bullish—a level historically associated with market tops. Conversely, institutional investors have been reducing equity exposure, with mutual fund cash levels rising to 3.8% as of July 2023, well above the historical average of 3.2%. This divergence between retail optimism and institutional caution creates a precarious situation where the market may be vulnerable to a sharp selloff if negative catalysts emerge. The recent rally has been driven largely by short-covering rather than fundamental buying, suggesting that any negative news could quickly erase these gains and accelerate the downtrend.
keyTakeaways: Key developments in It's A Bear Market Rally, affecting market outlook

However, the fundamental problems that initiated the previous corrective phase are still present. Such factors generally range from declining earnings to monetary condition changes, rising recession risk, economic policy uncertainty, or a combination that persists despite temporary price increases

Even with those criteria, though, investment professionals often disagree over whether the financial world is enjoying the beginning of a bull market or just a brief respite from a prolonged bear market.

### Historical examples

The most recent bear market started on February 19, 2020, during the COVID-19 global pandemic. The S&P 500 fell 27% from the highs and concluded hitting lows around March 24, 2021. The sheer magnitude of pace of the sell-off qualified it as a . Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of market analysis trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
